data.title: Zillow Home Value Index: 'Real' Home Values At Lowest Level Since March 2021
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Zillow, the real estate listing and brokerage website, provides a wealth of publicly available real estate data. Among these, the Zillow Home Value Index (ZHVI) offers a seasonally adjusted measure of home values and market trends. Its "flagship" ZHVI is based on data for "all homes, middle tier, smoothed and seasonally adjusted cut," aiming to represent the typical home value rather than the median. Introduced in 2006, the ZHVI includes various segmentations by geography and housing type. Here, we focus on the flagship ZHVI, which reflects typical home values.

Home values rose for the first time in seven months in September, according to the Zillow Home Value Index. However, after adjusting for inflation, real home values dropped for a 17th consecutive month, remaining at their lowest level since March 2021.

Last month's ZHVI came in at $363,932, up 0.07% from the previous month and up 0.10% from one year ago. However, after adjusting for inflation, the real figures are -0.31% month-over-month and -3.76% year-over-year.

Zillow Home Value Index Methodology

Zillow’s ZHVI is measured using monthly changes in its “Zestimates,” its proprietary home valuation model. The index is meant to be timely, comprehensive, and transparent, published three weeks after the close of a given month. Many other home value indexes are published with a significant time lag - like the S&P at CoreLogic Case-Shiller Index and the FHFA House Price Index.

ZHVI is calculated with a few assumptions:

  • Monthly changes are calculated with a weighted mean
  • The average Zestimate within a range determines the index level
  • It can be interpreted as the market’s total appreciation for a given geography or cut

In addition, historical appreciation is based on actual historical housing stock versus a fixed basket of homes, and home improvements are considered in appreciation unrelated to market movements.

The first chart below shows the flagship ZHVI series - the “typical home value” since 2000, not adjusted for inflation. The current nominal value is 0.69% off its all-time high from February 2025.

Next, we take a look at the same index values but adjust for inflation using the CPI. As you can see, it’s a different picture. According to the ZHVI, today’s home values are below that of the (inflation-adjusted) housing peak in 2006 and are currently 12.79% off its all-time high from July 2022.

Here is an overlay of the nominal and real indexes for a comparative look.

Housing Prices (ZHVI) and Mortgage Rates

The following charts overlay mortgage rates with both nominal and real ZHVI, highlighting their relationship. Typically, rising interest rates reduce buyer affordability, decreasing demand for homes. This can lead to downward pressure on home prices. With that said, today’s housing market is following a different path because we’re seeing elevated mortgage rates in conjunction with elevated home prices.

Zillow Home Value Index vs. S&P CoreLogic Case-Shiller Index

Zillow claims that the ZHVI are "the most accurate and timely measures of residential real estate prices in the United States" and that its index is more representative of housing stock than the S&P CoreLogic Case-Shiller Home Price Index. Zillow claims this is due to its extensive database that includes input from real homeowners as well as their sophisticated home valuation model in the Zestimate.

Zillow believes its ZHVI has distinct advantages over the Case-Shiller index. The Case-Shiller (CS) Index uses the repeat-sales methodology, meaning it measures price changes by looking at homes that have resold in a region, while the ZHVI only includes homes that have sold at least twice in recent history and excludes all new construction. Here are a few charts pulled directly from a piece by Zillow comparing the two indexes. We tried to get this data to do our own analysis, but the research group there would not provide it. Here’s a link to that article.

Another difference between the CS and ZHVI is that CS includes foreclosure resales. Zillow emphasizes that foreclosure resales are "substantially different from non-distressed sales" and are often considerably discounted from such. Zillow claims that using foreclosure resales represents "two very different market segments." Eric Stubbs, Ph.D., at RBC WM believes foreclosure sales is a matter of taste and says that foreclosures are actual sales, so they do in fact affect the price of surrounding properties.

In any case, the ZHVI is another tool in the tool belt to determine home values and is useful due to its timeliness and variety of information.

ETFs associated with residential real estate include: iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors

data.title: Politics And The Markets 10/18/25
data.description:
2This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation

  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

data.title: Bitcoin Is At Trend Support
data.description:
After what had been a truly terrific 500% rise since the start of 2023, I turned considerably more cautious on Bitcoin (BTC-USD) in July and downgraded the coin to a 'hold.' I reiterated the asset as a 'hold' through coverage of the ProShares Bitcoin Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:BITO) in August and again through the Franklin Bitcoin ETF (BATS:EZBC) in September - each time, taking a slightly different angle in my coverage.

After several months of chop, it appeared as though Bitcoin was ready for smooth sailing following a breakout to a nominal new all time high earlier in October. That breakout is proving to be a fake-out so far. As of article submission, Bitcoin is currently hanging on to support near $107k per coin after knifing through its 200 day moving average early in the October 17th session.

Recapping The Summer Downgrade

My primary concerns when I downgraded Bitcoin to a 'hold' in July could best be summed up into three main points:

  1. Lack of on-chain usage growth, "adoption" through ETF speculation
  2. Proliferation of DATs reminiscent of the 'Blockchain' mania in 2017
  3. Capital flows generally shifting to Ethereum (ETH-USD) over Bitcoin

As I see it, on-chain data continues to favor Ethereum over Bitcoin on several levels. First, the daily active user trend for Ethereum is positive, while Bitcoin peaked two years ago.

September was the second month where Ethereum bested Bitcoin by average DAAs - and this notably doesn't include L2 networks or scaling chains, where ETH has a significant advantage over BTC. Transactions favor ETH, and valuation does as well:

Despite Bitcoin retaking the lead in dollar-denominated transferred value from Ethereum in September, at current prices investors are paying more than a 3x premium for Bitcoin's network when measured by the 30-day average NVT ratio. From a capital flow standpoint, Bitcoin has had a significant resurgence against ETH in recent weeks:

Asset (mil) MTD Flows YTD Flows AUM
Bitcoin $5,128 $30,212 $188,694
Ethereum $1,071 $13,998 $36,456
Multi-asset -$51.6 -$49 $7,684
Solana $196.1 $2,670 $4,795
XRP -$50.9 $1,889 $2,941
Total* $6,343 $48,715 $242,503
BTC Dominance 80.8% 62.0% 77.8%
ETH Dominance 16.9% 28.7% 15.0%

Source: CoinShares, Bloomberg, through October 10th, *includes assets not shown in the table

So far through the first third of October, Bitcoin dominance in the capital flow game is better than 80%. At one point this summer, ETH dominance in YTD capital flows hit 34%; significant because ETH's real dominance ratio by market cap has been more range-bound between 12-15%. Still, at 28.7% year-to date flow dominance, Ethereum has been punching well above its weight this year. Again, Bitcoin is admittedly off to a better start in October. But when we look at fresher data from Farside, the reaction following the October 10th liquidation event has been to de-risk BTC to a larger degree:

$ in millions BTC ETF Flows ETH ETF Flows
13 Oct 2025 -$326.4 -$428.5
14 Oct 2025 $102.7 $236.2
15 Oct 2025 -$104.1 $169.6
16 Oct 2025 -$530.9 -$56.8
Weekly total -$858.7 -$79.5

Source: Farside Investors

Through Thursday October 16th, nearly $860 million has come out of Bitcoin investment products this week. It's less than $100 million for Ethereum. But the larger point is, as quickly as this liquidity can bid the price up, so too can it take the price down if investors shift to a risk-off sentiment. The other thing to consider is Bitcoin's whale buyer has all but stopped purchasing through October:

Through the first two weeks of October, Strategy's (MSTR)(STRF) Bitcoin acquisitions have totaled just $27 million. Although the company has an ability to raise capital for BTC purchased through its various preferred stock ATMs, more than 89% of the capital raised to buy BTC has come from the common stock ATM since company started selling preferred stocks in March. With basic mNAV compression progressively deteriorating from 2 to 1.18 since November, Strategy's easy road to BTC buys is running out of ground as every buy with preferred share capital creates a perpetual liability.

Open Interest And The Liquidation Event

I briefly mentioned the October 10th event in the prior section. During that liquidation, Bitcoin open interest declined from $90.2 billion to $70.5 billion in a single day. And in that major market deleveraging, Bitcoin saw a $20k high-to-low candle on numerous major exchanges, including Kraken, which actually experienced a $22k single-day drawdown.

While the coin had indeed bounced off lows and even tested $116k shortly thereafter, a major liquidation of this magnitude can certainly have an impact on BTC price further out. For the benefit of comparison, I'd like to show 1, 2, and 3-month returns for BTC following a few other single-day open interest meltdowns in what I'm calling the institutional post-COVID era:

Post-COVID Meltdowns May 19th 2021 September 7th 2021 December 4th 2021
BTC OI Decline $4.9b $4.4b $6.1b
BTC Price $37,341 $46,778 $48,204
1m Change -3.3% 15.4% -4.9%
2mo Change -17.5% 44.5% -14.2%
3mo Change 25.21% 10.48% -18.85%

Source: CoinGlass

To be clear, I'm tracking large declines in open interest, not single-day price declines. What this shows is the potential connection between significant declines in OI relative to how BTC performs shortly after. This table suggests that large declines in OI over a single day lead to mixed returns over the next several months. My assumption is the poor performance following the December 4th, 2021 OI decline is simply due to where Bitcoin was in its halving cycle.

Unless we're of the view that halving cycle theory is no longer relevant, I'd say Q4 2025 is about the time to be lightening up exposures. Given the liquidation even on October 10th, the nearly $20 billion single day decline in Bitcoin open interest, and our current position in the four year halving cycle, I suspect returns over the following few months will be more similar to the December 4th, 2021 returns than the other two examples.

Of course, there is a very real possibility that Bitcoin is simply testing long term trend support and will rebound from current levels. I'd be remiss if I did not mention that the 12 month MA has served as trendline support 3 times over the course of the last 15 months, and BTC just tagged that line again through Kraken. But the counter to that point would be that as each cycle progresses, Bitcoin builds more bearish RSI divergences. We can see this from the 2017 through 2025 cycles as well as from February 2024 to today.

Closing Takeaways

I want to be very clear; I still own Bitcoin. I'm still long BTC because I believe there remains a possibility that it can serve as a useful cross-border settlement asset. I do believe it will have a lot of competition there, with tokenized Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) on networks like Ethereum being a very formidable foe for Bitcoin's 'Digital Gold' narrative. Still, Bitcoin has something that no other digital asset has; first-mover advantage. And there's a lot of sticky value in that. However, none of this means I think Bitcoin is screaming 'buy' today. In fact, I'm becoming even more convinced that we've seen the top for this halving cycle already. From where I sit, the technicals aren't great, and the cycle is getting ripe. And purely speculating here, I suspect there is far more damage from the $19 billion liquidation event on October 10th than is currently understood. When Terra Luna collapsed in May 2022, it took 6 more months to figure out who all of the counterparty casualties were. The post-Luna period turned out to not be a good time to be long BTC, looking out the next 12-18 months. I have reduced my exposure to BTC through both the ETFs and the mining stocks.

data.title: Mammoth Energy Services: Increases Investments In Its Aviation Portfolio
data.description:
Mammoth Energy Services (NASDAQ:TUSK) has changed its business focus. It sold off some of its infrastructure subsidiaries (that accounted for around 85% to 90% of its infrastructure business) and its pressure pumping equipment for a combined total of $123.7 million, and has been investing in its aviation portfolio.

I believe that it received a good price for the sale of its infrastructure subsidiaries, but the sale of its pressure pumping equipment was underwhelming and also reduces Mammoth's upside in a recovery scenario due to its exit from the well completion services business.

Mammoth is a much smaller company now as a result of these sales and is attempting to reach breakeven free cash flow in the near-term.

Based on its projected cash on hand and its remaining businesses, I estimate Mammoth's value at $3.20 per share now. This is around $1 less than my prior estimate of Mammoth's value, as that higher valuation was largely dependent on a partial recovery in Mammoth's well completion services business.

Sale Of Pressure Pumping Equipment

Mammoth sold off its pressure pumping equipment for $15 million. This appears to be below book value, as Mammoth's pressure pumping (well completion services) division had $35 million in net property, plant, and equipment on its balance sheet at the end of 2024, which declined by $31 million to $4 million in net property, plant, and equipment at the end of Q2 2025 (post-divestiture).

Mammoth's well completion services division was once a major contributor to its results, generating $33.7 million in adjusted EBITDA in 2022 and $15.9 million in adjusted EBITDA in 2023. However, it reported negative $9.1 million in adjusted EBITDA in 2024 from that division.

Mammoth had previously been waiting for demand to pick back up, but it decided to exit that area with natural gas prices still around $3.

While the price it received for its equipment appears relatively low, much of its equipment was also getting outdated, and selling it removes the need for further spending on upgrades.

However, exiting this business also significantly reduces Mammoth's potential upside. Well completion services could have added $10 million to $20 million EBITDA to Mammoth's results in a recovery scenario.

Continuing Businesses And Investments

Mammoth has been investing in its aviation portfolio, spending $25 million year-to-date in that area. This includes the eight small passenger aircraft it purchased in April for around $11.5 million.

Mammoth is aiming for a 2x to 3x return on invested capital from its aviation investments over a three to five year time frame.

Mammoth expects negative $3 million to negative $4 million in adjusted EBITDA from its continuing operations in 2H 2025. This includes the impact of around $2 million to $2.5 million in legal fees (related to the Puerto Rico litigation).

Mammoth believes that declining legal fees after 2H 2025 plus aviation growth should then help it soon get to neutral free cash flow with its continuing operations.

Cash Position

Mammoth had $157 million in cash at the end of Q2 2025 (including $30 million in restricted cash). It also had $48 million in income taxes payable, so its cash position (net of income taxes payable) is $109 million.

Mammoth is owed $20 million from PREPA, payable within seven days of the effective date of PREPA's plan of adjustment with its bankruptcy proceedings.

Mammoth projects around $15 million in capex from continuing operations in 2H 2025. It also expects a $3 to $4 million EBITDA loss from continuing operations in the second half of the year and $4 million to $5 million in cash burn related to discontinued operations.

I am modeling Mammoth's year-end 2025 cash position at $89 million (net of income taxes payable). This is based on the above inputs plus several million in interest income generated by Mammoth's cash position.

The $89 million figure doesn't include the $20 million still due from PREPA, as that bankruptcy case continues to drag along. This also doesn't include any further proceeds from the sale of underutilized assets.

Notes On Valuation

Mammoth is projected to end 2025 with $89 million (or around $1.85 per share) in cash, net of income taxes payable. Adding the $20 million due from PREPA (and discounting that by 25% due to the uncertainty around payment timing) would bring this up to $104 million (or approximately $2.16 per share).

Adding $50 million in value for Mammoth's remaining businesses would bring its value up to $154 million (approximately $3.20 per share) at the end of 2025.

Mammoth has spent $25 million on its aviation portfolio so far this year, and much of its remaining $15 million capex budget is expected to be growth capex for its aviation portfolio and equipment rental business.

So given that it will have spent close to $40 million on acquiring assets during 2025, a valuation of $50 million for its continuing businesses may be conservative. However, Mammoth is expected to be at slightly negative to neutral adjusted EBITDA in the near-term.

Conclusion

Mammoth has divested much of its previous main businesses and is increasing its investments in its aviation portfolio now. It is projected to end 2025 with $89 million in cash net of income taxes payable now, and is also owed a further $20 million from PREPA.

This gives Mammoth a good financial position as it attempts to reach breakeven cash flow in the near-term. However, while the exit from the well completion services business reduces the variability of Mammoth's results, it also significantly reduces Mammoth's upside in a recovery scenario.

I now estimate Mammoth value at approximately $3.20 per share.

data.title: Regions Financial: Q3 Defies Credit Fears, But Upside Is Limited
data.description:
Shares of Regionals Financial (NYSE:RF) have been a mixed performer over the past year, losing about 3% of their value. Shares did trade slightly higher on Friday after the company reported solid results, which may calm some fears about credit quality. I last covered shares of Regions in July, rating the stock a “hold,” but given the recent tumult in the sector, a sell would have been justified given the 10% decline in shares. With updated financials, now is a good time to see if shares should be bought on this dip or if investors should hold off for now.

In the company’s third quarter, Regions Financial earned $0.63, which beat estimates by $0.03 as revenue grew by 4% to $1.94 billion. Balance sheet growth remains fairly modest, but slowly, fixed rate maturities are supporting net interest income, and fee revenue has also been strong. Importantly, credit quality is improving, despite the fears that have become pervasive in markets. Overall, results were solid.

Balance sheet growth is very muted

Average deposits rose by $200 million sequentially to $129.6 billion. This was up about 3% from last year, a solid performance in an environment with sparse loan growth as the Federal Reserve shrinks its balance sheet. Deposit costs were stable QoQ, but they are down only 21 bps from last year. This is a fairly weak pull-through of Fed rate cuts, reflecting the fact that RF had been offering comparatively low deposit rates. This had been a headwind for its deposit growth, but only passing on a small fraction of the 100 bps in Fed rate cuts to customers has brought pricing more in-line, enabling deposit growth to pick back up. I expect a ~30% beta to incremental rate cuts with a sequential drop in Q4 costs likely.

Regions had $96.6 billion in average loans, up about 0.5% from Q2. Loans were still down slightly from last year. Generating loan growth has been difficult for much of the banking sector as elevated rates and higher amounts of economic uncertainty diminish borrowers’ demand. The entire decline in balances over the past year has been due to an intentional decline in consumer borrowing. So far this year, it has exited $900 million of leveraged corporate loan exposure, with about $300 million more that it wants to offload, in an effort to reduce its exposure to high-risk credits.

Within its loan portfolio, it has $16 billion of commercial real estate, and at less than 20% of loans, this exposure is fairly low. About 30% of this exposure is in apartments, and while rents are off their peak, I do not see meaningful losses coming from this asset class. Only 1% of its total portfolio is in office. I do see the need to potentially add $25-$40 million in reserves here to cover losses, but these are manageable and will be spread out over several years.

Fixed reinvestment should gradually boost interest income

Net interest income (“NII”) was stable sequentially at $1.26 billion, which was up about 3% from last year. Regions’ net interest margin (“NIM”) compressed by 6 bps to 3.59%. This was still up 5 bps from a year earlier. Now, last quarter, it had a $9 million, or 3 bp, one-time benefit from recovering on a loan that previously had been nonaccrual. Additionally, RF had more low-rate fixed hedges this quarter than last quarter, providing another $5 million of headwinds. Otherwise, NII would have been up 1% sequentially, more consistent with the company’s underlying trend in my view.

Regions’ NIM has been weighed down by having fixed rate exposure at low yields. It has a $33 billion securities portfolio, which has a $1.2 billion unrealized loss. This loss should fall below $1 billion next year as bonds mature and pull to par. RF can redeploy maturities at higher yields to boost NIM. On top of this, it has significant fixed rate swap exposure. Next year, its swap exposure should boost NIM by 1-2 bps with a further 1-2 bps in 2027. It will be a slow recovery, but this balance structure does leave RF fairly well insulated from rate cuts.

Noninterest income was very strong in the quarter at $684 million, up over 5% from last year, led by a 9% jump in wealth management thanks to rising markets and a strong sales environment. Improved capital markets and debt issuance also drove a 12% increase in capital markets to $104 million. I expect wealth momentum to continue in Q4 while capital market activity may slow somewhat.

Credit quality has improved

Now, Regions took 55 bps of net charge-offs, which was up from 47 bps last quarter. At the same time, nonperforming loans have been falling and are down 6 bps from a year ago. These two movements are linked. Essentially, we are seeing loans that have been delinquent for longer move through the process and get charged off. However, importantly, fewer new delinquencies are starting, which is why NPLs are shrinking. This in turn should lead to lower charge-offs in the future. Essentially, RF is working through credit issues on its back book of loans, but more recently, performance is improving.

This should help to alleviate fears around credit quality that have come to dominate the regional banking sector. Given this improvement, Regions reserved $105 million for credit losses, down $21 million from last quarter and also $8 million better than a year earlier. As a result, allowances are down slightly at $1.7 billion. Even as reserves are lower than a year ago, they cover 226% of NPLs, higher than the 210% of a year ago. I like to see coverage closer to 250%, so reserves are a bit on the light side, but they are closer to where I like to see them. I expect RF to largely maintain reserves at this level rather than allow them to fall further.

In terms of capital, Regions had a 10.8% tier 1 common equity (CET1) capital ratio. This is up 20 bps from last year. However, this excludes its unrealized loss on securities, which sits in AOCI. Over time, these losses are likely to be factored into capital calculations. As bonds mature and pull to par, you can see that RF’s adjusted capital position is steadily improving, and it is now squarely inside the company’s targeted range at 9.5%. I view RF as having adequate, but not excessive, capital.

With capital where it needs to be, RF can focus more of its earnings on capital returns. I continue to view its 4.5% dividend yield as very secure. We are also seeing buybacks accelerate, with $251 million completed in the quarter. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down about 2.7% over the past year, and I expect about a 3% share count reduction over the next year as well.

Shares are reasonably valued, but multiple expansion is unlikely

Alongside results, RF narrowed its NII guidance to 3-4% from 3-5% previously while increasing its noninterest outlook to 4-5% from 2.5-3.5%. Management expects NII to accelerate 1-2% in Q4 as RF recovers its lost NIM in Q3.

Back in July, I was targeting about $2.25-$2.40 in EPS this year. I now see RF earning $2.32-$2.42. That leaves shares right around 10x earnings. I view regional banks as broadly in the fair value range of 9-12x earnings, depending on their capital position, loan quality, and growth. RF has slow growth, improving credit quality, and in-line capital. As such, I view shares as fair value in the middle of this range. Particularly with the fears surrounding regional banks, I view 10x as a fair multiple, and I do not expect meaningful multiple expansion (absent a broader rally in regional banks as a whole). Accordingly, I continue to see RF as a suitable hold, but I do not see sufficient upside to merit an upgrade to buy even after the recent underperformance.

data.title: Examining The S&P 500 High Dividend Index Amid Changes In Monetary Policy
data.description:
By George Valantasis

Whether it’s the beginning of the school year or the drop in temperatures, September is often seen as a month of change. This year, there’s an additional factor reinforcing this idea: on Sept. 17, 2025, the U.S. Federal Reserve reduced the Fed Funds rate to 4.00%-4.25%, representing the first change in monetary policy since late 2024. With this rate cut and the market pricing further cuts before the end of the year, high-dividend-yielding strategies like the S&P 500® High Dividend Index may garner increased interest among market participants.

Alongside the supportive monetary policy, the S&P 500 High Dividend Index currently exhibits both a significant valuation and a dividend yield advantage compared to the S&P 500. This blog will explore these factors, delve into long-term performance—especially during historical drawdown events—and examine the current sector weights in relation to The 500™.

Performance Comparison

Exhibit 1 presents back-tested historical performance dating back to January 1991. Across the period studied, the S&P 500 High Dividend Index outperformed The 500 by 13 bps, with returns of 11.24% compared to 11.11%, albeit with higher volatility. Notably, despite this increased volatility, the S&P 500 High Dividend Index had capture ratios below 100.

Exhibit 2 shows the performance of the S&P 500 High Dividend Index during 10 historical drawdowns since 1998. Although it underperformed during the Global Financial Crisis and the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic, the S&P 500 High Dividend Index outperformed The 500 in the other eight significant drawdown events over the full period. Across the 10 drawdowns, the S&P 500 High Dividend Index recorded an average return of -12.1%, while The 500 averaged -19.8%, meaning the former had an average outperformance of 7.7%.

Dividend Yield Comparison

Exhibit 3 illustrates the current last 12-month (LTM) dividend yields for both the S&P 500 High Dividend Index and The 500. As of Sept. 30, 2025, the S&P 500 High Dividend Index offered a yield of 4.52%, while The 500 yielded 1.17%, showing a notable difference of 3.34%.

Valuation Comparison

Exhibit 4 illustrates the composite valuation discount by employing a straightforward average of the price-to-book, price-to-sales and price-to-earnings ratios of the S&P 500 High Dividend Index in comparison to The 500. As of Sept. 30, 2025, the S&P 500 High Dividend Index was trading at a 52% discount, reflecting discounts of 65%, 58% and 32% on a price-to-book (P/B), price-to-sales (P/S) and price-to-earnings (P/E) basis, respectively. The 52% composite discount placed it in the 97th percentile of relative cheapness since 2007.

Sector Comparison

Exhibit 5 displays the current sector weights for The 500 and the S&P 500 High Dividend Index. The S&P 500 High Dividend Index demonstrates a notable underweight of 33.5% in the Information Technology sector. This underweight is counterbalanced by substantial overweights in the Real Estate, Consumer Staples and Utilities sectors, which have overweights of 20.4%, 11.6% and 10.7%, respectively.

Conclusion

The S&P 500 High Dividend Index may be of particular interest amid the easing monetary policy backdrop due to its historically favorable valuation and dividend yield relative to the S&P 500. The index’s relative valuations and high dividend yield are notable characteristics, particularly in the context of ongoing concerns about elevated valuations, low yields and concentration in broader benchmarks.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit S&P Dow Jones Indices. For full terms of use and disclosures please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Dell: The Hardware Engine Behind Enterprise AI Adoption
data.description:
Introduction:

In each of my prior articles on Dell Technologies (NYSE:DELL), I rated the stock a STRONG BUY and both have outperformed the broader market meaningfully over each of the respective timeframes (see here and here).

Since then, Dell has released new quarterly results and completed the annual analyst meeting, which have further solidified my bullish stance on the company. The company's infrastructure business is continuing to experience strong demand as companies shift AI workloads on-premises and continue to invest in building out storage, networking, and compute capacity.

The firm's PC business has also continued to perform moderately well, and I believe this segment is poised to benefit from a few key trends that should improve top line growth over the next few years.

Given the company's clear path towards margin expansion and substantially higher revenue growth, I am reiterating my STRONG BUY rating on Dell's stock.

Business Description & Key Considerations:

Dell builds and sells hardware systems through its Infrastructure Solutions Group (ISG) that are used by various companies to deploy AI models, which positions the company at the center of the single most popular investing trend at the moment.

AI technology is now driving roughly 45% of U.S. GDP growth and is projected to add $15 trillion to the global economy by 2030, which would help push worldwide GDP to roughly $150 trillion. Additionally, AI hardware and services spending is projected to hit $310 billion by 2027, which is more than double the prior estimates of $124 billion from a few years ago. Both of these trends should act as tailwinds for the stock given the company's pivotal role in helping firms build infrastructure to implement AI technology.

Dell is currently building custom systems with tens of thousands of GPUs for large players in the AI space such as CoreWeave (CRWV), xAI, and ServiceNow (NOW). The company is also capable of quickly delivering and installing current generation GB300 racks, which are equipped with the ultra-powerful Blackwell GPUs from NVIDIA (NVDA).

The firm's integrated racks and PowerEdge XE9712 servers are built to house and connect equipment designed by NVIDIA, with advanced liquid cooling systems and networking equipment pre-installed to ensure customers can begin using the infrastructure quickly after delivery.

Dell has also partnered with AMD (AMD) and Intel (INTC), which means customers have lots of optionality regarding which chip designer they want to purchase from depending on preferences and different use cases. The company also works with a number of frontier model developers and those who are creating software tools using AI that are able to be operated on top of the infrastructure Dell helps customers build.

So far, Dell is already working with over 3,000 large customers on AI-related initiatives, and over 85% of enterprises have or are planning to move at least some of their AI systems on-premises over the next couple of years. Given the fact that the majority of enterprise data is unstructured, companies that are looking to utilize generative AI models will need to invest in AI-optimized networking and storage solutions like those that Dell offers in order to optimize operations.

Speaking of storage solutions, Dell's mid-range storage system PowerStore is currently capable of delivering a 5-to-1 data reduction guarantee, which means customers will be able to store more data with less physical capacity. At the moment, over 17,000 customers use PowerStore and this product has delivered double-digit YoY revenue growth in each of the last five quarters.

An even more exciting innovation is Dell's AI Data Platform that is powered by both PowerScale and ObjectScale. This solution helps enterprise customers both manage and analyze unstructured data such as video footage and streams from IoT devices, which is especially useful for companies with physically intensive operations as well as those in data heavy industries such as healthcare and finance.

There is also a lot of opportunity for Dell within the other segment of the business, the Client Solutions Group (CSG). Over 500 million PC users require updated hardware to transition to new operating systems, which should act as a near-term tailwind for revenue growth. Also, the company is beginning to integrate neural processing units (NPUs) into its newest high end PC models, which are capable of running small language models (SLMs) locally instead of in the cloud.

Considering the fact that many everyday tasks that employees may need to complete using generative AI models are not incredibly computationally intensive, using less complex SLMs directly on devices is a more efficient, scalable, and safe option for enterprise clients. This is also a lower-cost option given that cloud AI inference costs can be a massive burden, even for deep-pocketed companies. Also, according to this source, AI PCs are expected to make up roughly 43% of all PC shipments this year.

Additionally, I believe that Dell's new Pro Essential line of computers will likely be in high demand from less established firms operating in developing economies as they are more affordable. At the moment, the Asia Pacific market is projected to see a 7% CAGR for PC sales from now through 2032, which supports my bullish view on this product in particular.

Finally, Dell is currently in the midst of a total revamp of its operations through the usage of AI systems and autonomous agents. The firm's vision appears to be to use these tools to manage repetitive work so that employees can focus on the more complex aspects of their jobs (such as high level engineering problems or creative work) rather than mundane tasks. Due to this shift, management is projecting that total operating expenses should decline as a percentage of revenue moving forward.

While I do believe that Dell's current positioning in the AI infrastructure space provides a strong growth runway, the company does face a few key risks which may impact performance. The biggest factor that may slow down growth for Dell is a decline in adoption of AI technology at the enterprise level.

According to a survey conducted by the Pew Research Center, only around 16% of U.S. employees are currently using AI applications for work, despite the fact that 91% are able to. While I personally believe this trend will flip over the coming years as useful tools proliferate, this is certainly a relevant statistic that is worth monitoring.

Also, the company is still subject to risks related to a broader macroeconomic slowdown or a decline in large-cap indexes, given Dell is a constituent of the S&P 500 (SPY). Again, I do believe that the company will fare better than many other names in the AI space, even if we enter a recession and subsequent bear market. I hold this opinion due to the fact that the company trades at a relatively cheap valuation, which I will expand on in the following section.

Financials & Valuation:

I'd like to begin this section by discussing the results of each segment of the business individually. The ISG delivered total net revenue growth of over 44% YoY, hitting $16.8 billion as sales attributable to the servers and networking business rose an astounding 66% to $12.9 billion. However, storage revenue declined roughly 3% YoY to $3.9 billion due to softer than anticipated demand.

Operating margins for the ISG segment declined 220 bps YoY to 8.8% due to a decline in gross profitability, which outpaced the decline in operating expenses for the segment as a percentage of sales. It should also be noted that Dell has sold $17.7 billion worth of AI servers throughout the first half of the year, which already dwarfs the $11 billion figure from last year.

The CSG segment saw total revenue rise just 1% YoY to $12.5 billion, with commercial revenue up a meager 2% to $10.8 billion due to an increase in average selling prices. Consumer revenue, on the other hand, saw revenue decline 7% YoY to just $1.7 billion as a result of the decline in the number of PCs sold during the quarter relative to Q2 FY25.

Operating margins for the CSG segment only saw operating margins decline by around 20 bps YoY to 6.4% due to an increase in R&D expenses as a percentage of sales for the unit, which I view as a necessary near term expense that should elevate growth rates for the business in the future.

Total net revenue rose 19% YoY to $29.8 billion in Q2 FY26, with gross margins declining roughly 310 bps to 18.3%. However, despite the decline in gross profitability and each of the two primary segments seeing operating margins contract on a YoY basis, company-wide operating margins actually improved slightly (up around 40 bps YoY to 6%).

This was largely due to declines in SBC, amortization of intangible assets, and other miscellaneous corporate expenses, along with the aggregate decline in SG&A expenses, which were down by 9.4% YoY. These improvements, along with diluted weighted shares outstanding declining by 5.2% YoY, contributed to the company's quarterly EPS figure rising 38.2% YoY to $1.70.

Finally, Dell is raising its revenue growth estimates for FY26-FY30 to approximately an 8% CAGR, with the ISG segment expected to deliver between 11-14% growth per annum and 2-3% growth for the CSG segment.

Management is also expecting to deliver EPS growth rates in excess of 15% on average in each of the next couple of years given the faster projected revenue growth for the ISG segment, which is a higher margin business. Dell is also using AI to automate many of the more laborious aspects of the business, which should lead to strong operating leverage and improved profitability over the coming years.

Now let's turn to the valuation of the stock at the present moment. In order to determine Dell's intrinsic value, I created a discounted cash flow (DCF) model. The perpetual growth rate I used in the model was 2.5%, and my projection period covers the next 10 years.

For my cost of capital, I first computed the cost of debt using the TTM interest expense, average total debt, and tax rate information that's publicly available. Next, I calculated the cost of equity using the yield on a 10-year U.S. Treasury as a proxy for the risk-free rate, the stock's beta from Yahoo Finance, and the equity risk premium listed on Professor Aswath Damodaran's website.

Below are my calculations for both the weighted-average cost of capital (WACC) and my discounted cash flow model:

For my sales estimates, I kept these roughly in line with consensus estimates and management's guidance for approximately 8% sales growth over the next half decade, with growth rates tapering off as adoption of AI server technology matures. For operating margins, I have projected a gradual expansion due to the expected mix shift toward Dell's higher margin ISG business over the coming years and operating leverage due to the implementation of AI technology across the enterprise.

I have also modeled D&A expenses to be around 2.3% of sales through FY30, which is roughly in line with the historical average over the past few years, and capital expenditures are slightly elevated over the same time horizon to reflect ongoing investments in various growth initiatives. After FY30, I decided to have both of these figures converge to 2.5% of sales.

Finally, for the change in net working capital (CNWC) estimates, I have set this to 3% of sales, as the historical figures have fluctuated wildly, and this is a notoriously difficult line item to predict.

Based on all of these assumptions, I arrived at an equity value of ~$224.4 billion, or an implied share price of $327.08. This represents incredible potential upside of over 185% based on the closing share price of $114.51 as of October 14th, 2025.

Conclusion:

I believe that Dell represents a fantastic investment opportunity at the moment given the bullish momentum in the AI hardware space along with key opportunities in the more mature CSG segment.

The current valuation of the stock appears to offer a substantial discount relative to Dell's fundamentals, and I believe that the company will be able to outperform management's revenue and earnings growth targets which should push shares higher over the coming quarters.

Overall, Dell remains a STRONG BUY in my opinion as there is lots of long term upside potential as the company remains a key player in the ongoing AI revolution.

data.title: This Week In Markets: Volatility, Trade Tensions, And Earnings Beats
data.description:
By Ryan J. Puplava, CMT, CTS, CES

The stock market endured another volatile but constructive week as investors navigated a mix of trade developments with China, a busy slate of earnings reports, and renewed concerns about financial fraud within regional banks.

Despite several sharp intraday swings, the major averages are set to end the week higher, supported by strength in technology and financials. Underneath the surface, however, sector breadth and rising volatility hinted at cautious sentiment ahead of the Federal Reserve’s upcoming policy meeting.

Trade War Developments and Market Reaction

Markets opened the week with strong gains as trade tensions between the United States and China appeared to ease after a tense period of escalating rhetoric. President Trump sought to calm investor nerves following last week’s threats of a 100 percent tariff on Chinese imports, declaring on Truth Social that “it will all be fine.”

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent added that both sides had resumed communication and that a meeting between Presidents Trump and Xi was still possible. The reassurance sparked a broad-based rally that lifted the S&P 500 by 1.6 percent and the Nasdaq by 2.2 percent on Monday, recovering roughly half of Friday’s losses.

Chipmakers led the advance, with the PHLX Semiconductor Index jumping nearly 5 percent after Broadcom (AVGO) announced a major partnership with OpenAI (OPENAI) to develop 10 gigawatts of custom AI accelerators. Nvidia (NVDA) also traded higher on news that Meta Platforms (META) and Oracle (ORCL) would use its Spectrum-X Ethernet networking switches in their AI data centers.

The optimism spread to mega-cap names, including Tesla (TSLA), which rose more than five percent. Smaller-cap indices outperformed as well, signaling renewed risk appetite across the market.

Tuesday, sentiment shifted again as China imposed sanctions on five U.S.-linked subsidiaries of South Korea’s Hanwha Ocean, reversing Monday’s optimism. Despite the headline, investors largely treated the move as limited retaliation, and the market managed to recover from early losses on Tuesday.

Treasury Secretary Bessent later suggested that a longer truce could be achieved if China delays its export restrictions on rare earth materials, helping the S&P 500 return to near-record levels on Wednesday before fading into the close. By Thursday, President Trump characterized the ongoing standoff as a “trade war,” which contributed to a late-week risk-off move that sent volatility higher.

Nonetheless, markets stabilized Friday on reports that Secretary Bessent and Chinese Vice Premier He Lifeng would resume direct trade talks, helping stocks finish the week modestly higher.

Investment Implications: Markets remain highly sensitive to trade rhetoric, with AI and semiconductor stocks acting as early barometers of sentiment. Investors should expect short-term volatility to persist but view any meaningful progress in negotiations as supportive for cyclical sectors, including industrials and technology suppliers tied to global trade.

Earnings Season Kicks Off on a Strong Note

The third-quarter earnings season began with encouraging results from the banking sector. Wells Fargo (WFC) and Citigroup (C) both beat expectations, with Wells Fargo surging over seven percent on Tuesday. JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and Goldman Sachs (GS) also reported strong numbers, though profit-taking trimmed some of their early gains.

Midweek, Morgan Stanley (MS) and Bank of America (BAC) joined the list of outperformers, helping to reinforce the market’s view that large banks remain resilient. Without any federal economic news, the earnings results from the banks were supportive for the overall economy.

Overall, earnings data have been robust. According to FactSet as of this week, 12 percent of S&P 500 companies have reported results, with 86 percent delivering positive EPS surprises and 84 percent topping revenue estimates. The blended (combining actual results and estimates) year-over-year earnings growth rate stands at 8.5 percent, marking the ninth consecutive quarter of earnings expansion for the index, according to FactSet.

The results underscore steady corporate profitability even in a slowing macro environment. Notably, technology continues to be a key earnings driver, with AI-related companies at the center of attention. Strong results from Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) and Broadcom’s new partnership with OpenAI underscored how AI infrastructure continues to make semiconductors the focal point of this earnings season.

While enthusiasm around technology remained high, there were signs of fatigue. Oracle’s AI World Conference disappointed investors on Friday, as management’s plans to ramp up spending raised margin concerns. The stock fell nearly seven percent, reflecting the market’s sensitivity to profit pressures even in growth-driven sectors.

Retailers and consumer discretionary names also saw strength earlier in the week, with Best Buy (BBY), Ross Stores (ROST), and Burlington (BURL) outperforming on renewed optimism that trade tensions would ease.

Investment Implications: Strong corporate earnings continue to justify higher equity valuations, particularly in the technology and financial sectors. However, margin compression and cost pressures remain a concern, suggesting that investors should balance growth exposure with defensive positions in cash flow-stable industries such as healthcare and consumer staples - one of my key strategies for client portfolios.

Economic Data and Rate Cut Expectations

Economic releases were sparse this week due to the ongoing government shutdown, but the data that did emerge painted a mixed picture. The Federal Reserve’s Beige Book showed that overall economic activity was little changed, with consumer spending softening modestly and manufacturing activity varying across regions. Employment levels remained steady while prices continued to rise at a gradual pace.

Regional manufacturing data were also uneven. The Empire State Manufacturing Index rebounded sharply to 10.7 in October from -8.7 in September, suggesting an improvement in business sentiment.

However, the Philadelphia Fed survey fell sharply to -12.8 from 23.2 the prior month, signaling potential weakness in that region. Housing indicators improved modestly, with the NAHB Housing Market Index climbing to 37, though mortgage applications continued to decline, reflecting affordability constraints.

Despite the mixed signals, markets remain confident that the Federal Reserve will cut rates later this month. Fed Chair Jerome Powell stated that downside risks to the labor market have increased, while Boston Fed President Susan Collins reiterated that modest rate cuts are warranted as inflation cools and hiring slows.

The CME FedWatch Tool shows a 99 percent probability of a 25-basis-point cut at the October FOMC meeting. Treasury yields reflected this conviction, with the two-year note yield falling to 3.43 percent, its lowest level since August 2022.

The ten-year yield settled near 4 percent at the time of this article. The rate outlook, coupled with the government shutdown’s data delays, has kept investors leaning on corporate earnings and trade developments for near-term direction.

Investment Implications: With a high probability of a Fed rate cut this month, income-sensitive assets such as bonds, utilities, and real estate could see renewed support. A lower policy rate may also sustain equity valuations, though investors should remain attentive to inflation trends and the potential for policy missteps as the Fed balances growth and price stability.

Banking Fraud and Financial Sector Concerns

The financial sector faced renewed scrutiny this week after several regional banks disclosed losses related to fraudulent commercial real estate loans. Zions Bancorp (ZION) and Western Alliance Bancorp (WAL) revealed that they were victims of loan fraud tied to funds investing in distressed properties, prompting sharp selloffs in both stocks on Thursday.

Zions reported a $50 million charge-off that will appear in its third-quarter earnings statement. The news sent the KBW Regional Bank ETF (KBWR) down over six percent that day, its steepest decline in months.

The revelations added to broader concerns about credit quality within the industry. Jefferies recently disclosed exposure to the bankrupt First Brands, while JPMorgan Chase and Fifth Third (FITB) were linked to the failed Tricolor Auto.

These incidents, combined with the new fraud cases, triggered a broader selloff in financials during the midweek session. However, sentiment improved by Friday as the sector recovered part of its losses, aided by stronger-than-expected earnings from Truist (TFC), Fifth Third, Comerica (CMA), and American Express (AXP).

Investment Implications: The recent fraud disclosures highlight ongoing vulnerabilities within regional banks, particularly those exposed to commercial real estate. Investors should exercise caution in the space and favor well-capitalized, diversified institutions over smaller lenders with concentrated loan portfolios. Select opportunities may emerge as valuations reset, but risk management remains paramount.

Chart of the Week

This week marks the start of earnings season, so it’s fitting that the chart of the week highlights that theme. According to FactSet, earnings growth remains heavily concentrated among the “Magnificent 7,” a trend that has persisted for several years.

However, with falling interest rates, increased capital spending, and the consumer boost Piper Sandler expects from tax relief in 2026, FactSet projects that earnings growth will begin to broaden across the remaining 493 companies in the S&P 500.

A wider base of earnings strength could help extend market performance beyond a handful of mega-cap and technology names. Monitoring cyclical sectors and their relative performance against the S&P 500 will be key in the months ahead to identify any potential inflection point as investors start to discount improving economic conditions.

Source: FactSet, “Earnings Insight,” October 17, 2025

Conclusion

This week’s trading underscored how quickly sentiment can shift amid conflicting headlines. The easing of trade tensions with China early in the week gave way to renewed friction by Thursday, though the dialogue remains open.

Strong earnings and stable rate expectations continue to support equities, but the rise in financial fraud cases and volatility in regional banks highlight ongoing risks beneath the surface.

For investors, the resilience of earnings and the high likelihood of a Fed rate cut suggest continued near-term support for risk assets, particularly in technology and consumer sectors tied to AI and digital infrastructure.

However, the market’s underlying caution, seen in declining breadth and rising volatility, suggests that diversification and attention to credit quality remain prudent as global and domestic uncertainties persist.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ServiceNow: A Defensive Tech Play With AI-Driven Growth Revival
data.description:
With a market likely to pressure valuations where narratives are not supportive, ServiceNow, Inc''s (NYSE:NOW) maturing profile becomes a strong reason for its positioning as defensive tech now. The last one year has been roughly flat and now with valuation pressures, as well as a psychological resistance of $1000, we could be looking at a Buy thesis that crucially hinges on the entry point at this moment.

The fundamentals, as we will see in this analysis, are still strong. Valuations justify the fundamentals despite looking expensive - although growth assumptions need to be monitored for execution and tech risks. All in all, at around $900, I would recommend a starter position in ServiceNow - a Buy with caution candidate with moderate return expectations.

Revenue Growth and Quality

Despite the scale of above $3 billion in quarterly revenues, ServiceNow's growth at scale remains impressive. In fact, Q2 2025 saw a rebound in growth with YoY growth improving to ~22% after slipping below 20% in Q1. The rebound in Q2 is largely attributable to ServiceNow's AI integrated platform focus that drove over 50% uptick in AI related deals.

This surge is a very strong validation of ServiceNow's AI strategy and innovation roadmap. Upgrading workflows to enable predictive analytics, automated recommendations and virtual agents is now no longer a moat, but a requirement for enterprise workflow solutions to remain relevant. ServiceNow's AI roadmap looks clear and tangible. The acquisition of AI startup Moveworks, for example, is expected to enhance ServiceNow's AI capabilities, particularly in customer service management. There are similar partnerships and smaller acquisitions that ServiceNow actively pursues to keep its AI roadmap updated. A broadening AI enhancement spilling over from IT services into customer service, HR, and security appears like enough AI related upgrades in the tank to allow for decent reinvigorated growth levels, like seen in Q2.

ServiceNow's strengths in terms of a recurring revenue friendly subscription model are already well appreciated and absorbed into valuations. Almost 97% of the revenues are a high quality recurring revenue, and that has been the case for quite a few quarters now, indicating ServiceNow's maturity as a SaaS model.

Future revenue visibility is also extremely strong with Q2 remaining performance obligations of $23.9b representing a 29% YoY growth. Revenue quality indicators also look excellent. The number of customers with an ACV of over $20m grew 30% YoY. Renewal rates are around 98% and mitigate the "larger than average" customer cohort renewal risk in Q4 2025.

Overall, revenue growth has revived and the AI tailwinds are clearly leading those gains, inspiring confidence in further AI led upsells and near term monetization. Other aspects of customer quality from retention to revenue visibility is a continuation of the bullish thesis that has supported ServiceNow for a while now.

Margin Outlook

The free cash flow margin sits at around the early thirties, pushing the combined margin-revenue growth metric sum over 50, almost elite SaaS territory and very impressive for a maturing SaaS player. The impressive cash conversion opens up reinvestments into further AI enhancements and R&D. Eventually, the AI wave could open the next leg of gross margin uptick (by reducing service delivery and support costs over time), which is otherwise quite healthy already, hovering around the 80% mark.

While gross margins have broadly saturated over the past few years, operating margin has improved with scale, with the latest improvements from 2022-23 pushing operating margins from low single digits to the teens now. ServiceNow is scaling efficiently, making the case for a tech play that has become both reasonably profitable and capital efficient. That with a 20%+ growth profile makes ServiceNow's case that of what makes for a strong defensive bet.

I expect the operating margins to continue to grow up to ~15% over the next 1-2 years. Gross margins may start to move over 80% too, after a few more quarters of AI monetization. Even if FCF conversion stagnates where it is today, ServiceNow's bull thesis (fundamentally speaking) still looks strong.

Valuation - High but Fair

In terms of valuations, ServiceNow's EV to forward revenue ratio has now contracted to ~14x, down from ~20x in 2024 (also slightly below the 5 year average of ~15.2x). However, in comparison with other enterprise SaaS leaders like Workday, Inc. (WDAY) and Salesforce, Inc. (CRM), the premium is now higher than in the recent past. This is why I think that despite the valuation differential with peers, and that in an environment where I myself have argued that valuation premiums will be challenged, ServiceNow is valued fairly.

  • ServiceNow is growing at a different scale compared to peers. Salesforce clocked a sub 10% growth in Q2. Workday grew by ~12% in Q2.
  • Salesforce and Workday do not show superior cash flow conversion. In fact the revenue growth-margin number is elite for ServiceNow, even in comparison with peers who barely cross the 40 mark.

  • ServiceNow's offerings address a broader TAM compared to Salesforce's CRM focus and Workday's HCM/finance focus.
  • A tangible and early validation of its AI strategy keeping its competitive position intact
  • There is scope for operating margin gains compared to more mature defensive enterprise SaaS companies like Salesforce.
  • My broad market outlook is not in contradiction to my ServiceNow Buy thesis. I believe valuations will be challenged wherever it is narrative driven and has less supportive evidences to show. ServiceNow has measurable and tangible narratives.

Risks, Outlook and Still a Buy

The risks to ServiceNow's Buy thesis are not beyond the average tech risks like an assumed tech relevance that keeps its competition at bay. Investors will need to keep an eye on not only ServiceNow's progress, but inroads made by competition that now also includes Microsoft in addition to other strong enterprise SaaS players like Salesforce and Workday.

The assumed growth rate in the high teens over the next several years also elevate the execution risks and requires ServiceNow to be on top of its competition for a sustained period of time. That is not under threat right now, but requires monitoring. As of now, a downtrend on R&D spends shows plateauing AI spend for every revenue gained. The R&D spend still remains healthy, helped by a strong cash flow conversion that helps drive R&D ramp-ups (like that seen from pre-COVID to around 2023 where ServiceNow heavily invested in AI and cloud initiatives).

ServiceNow has not had a history of significant revenue surprises. I don't expect revenue surprises in Q3 either, with revenue likely to come in somewhere above ~$3.35 billion. Non-GAAP EPS may come in better than the expected $4.27 mark on the back of AI synergies starting to show up in margins. I will keep an eye on the sub-plots over the headline beats though. ServiceNow's Buy thesis hinges on ongoing AI traction and that leading to margin benefits (although the margin benefits have a couple of quarters more to start showing up meaningfully, in my opinion).

Overall, I recommend a cautious Buy before the earnings results and expect better than expected EPS and margins to support short term upsides. Long term investors can expect single digit returns from this defensive stock, entering at current levels. The thesis only breaks if ServiceNow clocks growth below 18% consistently - indirectly pointing towards other systemic faults like AI monetization and margin stagnation. But as of now, all looks good for long term patient capital.

data.title: High Yield And Tax Advantaged Income Funds From NEOS
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In recent months, NEOS Investments has launched some high yield, tax efficient exchange-traded funds ("ETFs") which are unique and compelling. In essence, NEOS takes index funds and cash then uses options to generate income on the volatility of the fund. As expected, the more volatile an index fund, the more it generates in income. What’s unique about NEOS is their use of section 1256 option contracts which have the distinct advantage of having distributions taxed at 60% long term and 40% short term capital gains, vs. the usual ordinary income rates on most option contracts. Years ago as I was trading options, I discovered these 1256 contracts, however the size of the contracts in amounts such as $1.8 million were too large for an individual to trade.

NEOS offers 13 ETFs which can be broken into three segments: high income, hedged equity income, and enhanced income. In this article I focus on the seven high income funds broken into equity (4) and alternatives (3).

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the ETFs are:

  • Monthly distributions with high yields generating substantial income payments.
  • Tax efficiency using the 1256 Index option contracts being taxed at 60% long term.
  • Upside of capital appreciation from the index fund.
  • Diversification using multiple funds focused on different sectors.
  • Professional management with experience to manage the options trades (vs. do it yourself).
  • Public listings with liquidity, easy to trade, given size and volume of trading.

NEOS Investments

Based in Westport, Connecticut, the company was founded by Troy Cates and Garrett Paolella. Both have decades of experience in trading options and managing ETFs. The name NEOS stands for “Next Evolution Options Strategies.” According to their website:

NEOS ETFs aim to deliver the next evolution of options strategies, where seeking income is the outcome. Built on decades of research and experience, NEOS ETFs aim to empower the investor with portfolio building blocks to provide high income, tax efficiency, and diversification through data-driven options-based ETFs.

The benefits of options-based ETFs include high monthly income generated from writing options, a lower correlation to traditional investment such as stocks and bonds, and finally tax efficiency using index options classified as Section 1256 contracts, which are taxed at 60% long term, 40% short term capital gains vs. ordinary income.

Most of the distributions are treated as return of capital (ROC) which lowers the cost basis of the investment. Transactions are listed in Form 1099 statements which will be sent out by brokerage firms. This return of capital can be tax efficient as the investor won’t be taxed until they sell the investment.

NEOS High Income ETFs

The table below shows the relevant metrics for each fund, separated into equity focused ETFs first, then Alternative investments.

EQUITY HIGH INCOME

The following four funds utilize Index options classified as section 1256 contracts, which are more tax efficient using the 60/40 tax rates.

NEOS S&P 500(R) High Income ETF (SPYI) is similar to investing in the equity make up of the S&P 500 Index (SP500). Income is generated by a combination of call option strategies (buying and selling) using SPX Index options (SPX) and tax loss harvesting. As a secondary goal, the fund may generate capital appreciation in rising markets but will most likely lag the actual S&P500. The majority (96%) of fund distributions are classified as return of capital. The next ex-dividend date is 10/22/25.

NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF (QQQI) is more volatile than SPYI given its heavier weighting to technology stocks, modeling the equity makeup of the Nasdaq-100 Index (QQQ). Income is generated by a combination of call option strategies (buying and selling) using NDX Index options and tax loss harvesting. As a secondary goal, the fund may generate capital appreciation in rising markets but will most likely lag the actual Nadaq-100. The majority (99% recently) of fund distributions are classified as return of capital. The next ex-dividend date is 10/22/25.

NEOS Russell 2000 High Income ETF (IWMI) is focused on small cap stocks using the Vanguard Russell 2000 ETF (VTWO). Income is generated by a combination of call option strategies (buying and selling) using the Russell 2000 (RTY) Index options and tax loss harvesting. As a secondary goal, the fund may generate capital appreciation in rising markets. The majority (85% recently) of fund distributions are classified as return of capital. The next ex-dividend date is 10/22/25.

NEOS ETF Trust - NEOS MSCI EAFE High Income ETF (NIHI) invests in global stocks ex-U.S., using the iShares Core MSCI EAFE ETF (IEFA). Income is generated by a combination of call option strategies (buying and selling) using Index options and tax loss harvesting. As a secondary goal, the fund may generate capital appreciation. The majority (100% recently) of fund distributions are classified as return of capital. The next ex-dividend date is 10/22/25.

HIGH INCOME ALTERNATIVES

The next three funds do not use the Section 1256 Index contracts.

NEOS Bitcoin High Income ETF (BTCI) takes advantage of the high volatility in Bitcoin (BTC-USD) using options to generate income. The fund invests in Bitcoin ETPs for capital appreciation and writes calls on Bitcoin Futures ETFs. Specifically it invests in Vaneck Bitcoin ETF (HODL) which accounts for 22.83% of the portfolio, and iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT) at 1.37%. The majority (95% recently) of fund distributions are classified as return of capital. The next ex-dividend date is 10/22/25.

Full disclosure, this is the first fund I’ve ever invested in that holds Bitcoin or any type of cryptocurrency. The way I saw it, this fund generates income primarily from the volatility of Bitcoin, which makes sense in my view. Therefore, I see it as an options trading vehicle more than a speculative bet on a relatively new asset class. The actual Bitcoin holdings only account for 24.2% of the portfolio. This means that if Bitcoin fell in value, the fund could still make money off the volatility.

NEOS Real Estate High Income ETF (IYRI) invests in U.S. listed real estate investment trusts that track the Dow Jones U.S. Real Estate Capped Index. For example, the top 3 holdings are Welltower Inc (WELL), Prologis (PLD), and American Tower Corp. (AMT). Income is generated by call option strategies. As a secondary goal, the fund may generate capital appreciation. The majority (79% recently) of fund distributions are classified as return of capital. The next ex-dividend date is 10/22/25.

This fund is one of the newer ETFs launched in January 2025. While I’m heavily invested in REITs, the 11.1% distribution yield is higher than any other fund I own. Therefore, I will consider switching some of the laggards to IYRI.

NEOS Gold High Income ETF (IAUI) is attractive to investors that would like to hold gold but are concerned that it doesn’t generate income and may fall in value. The fund invests in physical gold for capital appreciation and generates income on options strategies using Gold ETPs. Specifically the fund holds 24.34% of the portfolio in Goldman Sachs Physical Gold ETF (AAAU). The majority (90% recently) of fund distributions are classified as return of capital. The next ex-dividend date is 10/22/25.

Performance

An investment of $10,000 in SPYI from inception in 2022 would have yielded a total return of $16,680 today. In comparison, an investment in the S&P500 - SPY would have yielded $19,250, showing an investor gives up some return as call options are exercised.

Similarly, an investment of $10,000 in QQQI over the past approximate two years, yields $13,790 vs. the NASDAQ index - QQQ at $14,240.

Risks

The stock market is currently trading at record level highs with many highly visible stocks trading at rich valuations. The potential for a correction is high, in my view. Although the market is shrugging off concerns about a government shutdown, it could prove to be a catalyst for a sell-off if not settled in a timely manner.

Geopolitical risks are still high around the world as the war continues in Ukraine as Trump was unable to broker a deal with Russia, and Russian war planes have flown over NATO countries. The risks include military action which could disrupt oil prices and supply chains.

Within each ETF the option portion caps some of the upside on each fund, so an investor needs to understand that long term, the total return would be higher if one just bought and held the index.

Expectations of a second and third rate cut in 2025 are high. As both inflation and unemployment are rising, the Fed may be pressured to hold rates if inflation rose too high as a result of tariff policy. That change in rate projections would alter the valuations of stocks that stood to benefit from a lower cost of borrowing.

Bottom Line

I recommend NEOS funds to income focused investors seeking high monthly distributions with potential for capital appreciation. The NEOS portfolio of high income ETFs offer significant benefits in the form of income generation from option strategies, potential tax efficiency, lower volatility, and total returns.

The funds are especially interesting because the distribution rates are high, in my view. For example, QQQI is currently yielding 14% vs. JPMorgan’s (JEPQ) at 10.46%. NEOS generates income from trading options on the underlying security. These index options used on the Equity High Income ETFs are classified as section 1256 contracts, which are subject to lower taxes at 60% long term and 40% short term capital gains. Overall, the goal is to use their ETFs for equity allocations, such as the S&P 500, Nasdaq, real estate etc. realizing there will be higher income but lower capital appreciation making up total return.

In the comment section below, I would be interested to hear you thoughts about these funds and potential fit within your portfolio.
